one free from propinquity, not uttering vain words, full of dharana and enthusiasm, unaffected in success and failure
passionate, desiring fruit, greedy, violent, impure, affected by joy and sorrow
renunciation; sannyasis are those who have given up desire for fruit of karma
the enumeration of principles that annul karma
Vasudev, the inner witness
demonic tendencies focused on body preservation and sensory gratification
dormant states of consciousness, minimal awareness
substances or impressions that affect the body and mind, also symbolic of mental dispositions
yogic practices aimed at awakening consciousness
selfless action or giving up attachments
hints from ancient sages, encoded methods of yoga
those who had vision of timeless reality
the field of yoga, containing the group of spirits (earth, water, fire, air, sky)
the five elements (earth, water, fire, air, sky) present in the body
the seat of immense consciousness, yet limited by body identification
the motivation derived from one's level of consciousness
desire to awaken immense consciousness beyond physical limits
obsession with body and senses, self-gratification
dormant consciousness, acting without purpose, minimal awareness
one established in samādhi, desireless
a realized master who knows the immense (Brahman)
vital energy, preservation is key for awakening
The ultimate Supreme Lord; the unmanifest great eternal Time; object of meditation
The consciousness that must be concentrated on the Time; awakened by breaking physical barriers

decryption_keys.symbol: body and senses (limitations of the body and the senses)
decryption_keys.meaning: That which withhold the mind and cause sin of ignorance; the limitations to be given up
Duties performed by the body; to be placed upon the Time
Consigning devotion to the Time Itself
Bow with the combined consciousness of past, present, and future
The medium through which one becomes dear to the Time
The practice of awakening the immense consciousness and focusing on the Time
One who has no mind for the Truth, averse to the Time due to indolence

decryption_keys.symbol: devotee (one who is a devotee of the Time)
decryption_keys.meaning: One who is eager to know the truth of the Time
One who does not even have a gleam of his latent immensity and is unwilling to know it
One who gives advice of the secret discipline to individuals eager for the truth

decryption_keys.symbol: beholding the truth of the Time in every embodiment
decryption_keys.meaning: Perceiving the Time in all forms; leads to becoming one with the Time
physical limitations of body and mind
immense consciousness / dormant consciousness
yoga practice to awaken immense consciousness
ignorance, desire for senses, hypocrisy, pride
initial flaws of yoga practice before realization
state of no karma, realization of unmanifest Truth
placed in ultimate immensity, knowing Time
direct observation of Time and realization of its sovereignty
becoming one with Time
the luminous, brilliant, unmanifest Governor
yoga practice surrendered to Time
physical limits, death fortress
limited intellect, preference for sense pleasures
resolve to know death and awaken consciousness
unmanifest Governor, brilliance in heart
seat of Ishvara in every creature
the unmanifest Time
physical limitations and ignorance
realization beyond death
class of humanity that resides between Israel and India who are Caucasian but not the white; those with sattvic qualities
the white race; those with rajasic qualities like bravery, lordship
the Mongol race; those with tendencies for agriculture, trade
the black race; those with service as natural duty
the quality that leads to awakening of consciousness, knowledge of Time
the quality of attachment to senses and physical limitations
the quality of dormant consciousness and ignorance
joy from yoga practice that initially feels like poison but leads to immortality
joy from sense gratification that initially seems nectar but becomes poison
joy from slumber, laziness, delusion
intellect that knows initiative and resignation, appropriate and inappropriate, fear and fearlessness, bondage and freedom, and motivates to awaken dormant consciousness
narrow intellect that cannot know the correlation of body and senses, does not comprehend death, cannot discern right action
intellect enveloped by ignorance, confuses dharma and adharma, afraid of death, grasps opposite meanings of wise teachings
resolute intellect from yoga that holds unmanifest immensity, stimulates knowledge of Time through union of mind and prana
holding limited to body, desires for sense satisfaction, unable to give up attachment
holding of slumber, fear, sorrow, depression, life like a dream
the source from which creation is manifested
witnessing consciousness (implied as Krishna in other texts)
The omnipresent unmanifest Brahma that yogis can know through successful practice of the three stages of ancient yoga; signifying symbol of the unmanifest truth of Time.
The consciousness of the Void in which is instilled the consciousness of Time, connoted by the word 'Tat'; it pervades everywhere.
Symbolic word used by great men to denote the unmanifest Time immanent in the Void; the one and only truth is unmanifest Time.
All karma that strengthens physical limitations, performed with desire for sense satisfaction by those with limited consciousness who presume the dormant immense consciousness as fictitious.
Bestowal of the advice of yoga to deserving seekers; also giving up fruits of karma.
The practice of yoga disciplines; continuing to inspire new seekers through penance of giving yoga advice.
The state of having risen above physical limitations, awakening dormant consciousness; knowing true nature of death means knowing the unmanifest, imperishable Brahma, the Time beyond mind.
Giving up the fruit of all karma; becoming desireless by banishing limitedness through yoga-karma.
Establishment in the knowledge that all desires originate from mind, causing desires to sink in mind; knowing the creator of all, the unmanifest Time.
Great yogis who realized the truth of Time and achieved oneness with It; those who are Time Conscious.
The procedures of yoga leading to knowledge of unmanifest Truth, manifested from the unmanifest truth of Time.
Correlated with mind; desires originate from mind, and the body and senses are instruments.
Bestowal of yoga advice to deserving seekers, by those who have become Time Conscious.
Charity done with expectation of reciprocal favors or gains, in a hurtful manner; give-and-take mentality.
Charity to undeserving, at inappropriate time/place, without courtesy; harming self and others.
Performing karma as duty without attachment and fruit; equanimity.
Giving up yoga-karma due to fear of physical discomfort, even knowing its importance.
Giving up yoga lessons out of delusion due to attachment to physical pleasures.
The ultimate imperishable Brahma, creator of all; the one and only truth; beyond the mind.
The direct darshan of the unmanifest Time by great men established in the Void.
Not explicitly defined but implied as faith in the dormant immense consciousness; without faith acts become Asat.

core_principles: The root consciousness of matter is Brahma
The void contains and fosters matter; its consciousness is Vishnu
Pure thought (consciousness of matter) is born from conscious void and links matter and void
Time (Shiva) is immanent in both matter and void, and annihilates both
Time existed before creation and will remain after dissolution
Time itself caused conception to Time, giving birth to creation
The body is an envelope of ignorance that serves to develop consciousness to its totality.
Consciousness is delivered through physical death after full development.
Time (Mahakal) is the creator, supporter, and annihilator of the world.
The one Self appears as the many through the play of time.
Imagination is the most powerful force; controlled imagination is will power.
The body is the field of yoga and a womb for consciousness.
Time is the origin of everything and appears dreamlike initially.
Practice begins with imagination and builds confidence.
The conscious void gives rise to creation and absorbs it back.
The body's capacity follows the state of realization.
A yogi becomes like Shiva by knowing Shiva through mind and body.
Supreme Time (Mahakal) manifests all and is the essence of life.
Prana (Shiva) is naturally related to all entities.
The activities of Prana and Apana are mutually opposite; their quiescence leads to realization of Mahakal.
All doubts originate from Time and are absorbed back into Time when one is united with Time.
The body is biological and the brain has four directional parts corresponding to physical locations.
Kundalini is consciousness and resides in the earth element (muladhara).
Physical development impacts the mind; internal yogic processes require physical prerequisites.
Khechari protects the practitioner from external dangers (e.g., explosions).
Holding purified prana for 48 minutes enables flying.
Amrita is a hormone produced by prolonged successful practice.
Prana is mercury, apana is sulfur; alchemical union purifies prana.
The physical body is a sentence of time for the spirit to feel time.
The spirit is a drop of time, infinite but needing contraction to feel time.
Observation power leads from gross to void to consciousness to time to Self.
Void is not empty but ocean of consciousness; within that flows time.
Mercury is the semen of Shiva, carrying divine DNA/memories.
Earth element is real Shakti; Kundalini sleeps in muladhar as earth.
Awakening Kundalini yields a yogic/divine/superbody by harmonizing five elements.
Mahamudra is a furnace that makes mercury heat-resistant; essential for 18 processes.
After first Kundalini awakening, one becomes aware of elements and 52 syllables; chakras are revealed.
Second Kundalini awakening makes one immortal and beyond chakras.
Body is 99.9% void; merging void with time allows absorbing time.
Physical body is a great mystery carrying immortal spirit; not to be discarded.
The One Self (Narayana) sleeps in the brain; 98% of brain is dormant but awakening is possible.
Time is not linear for ojas formation; it is individual and can extend lifespan.
Life force (sexual) can be transformed into ojas through celibacy and yoga practice (assimilation, not mere retention).
The body's cells are replaced every 12 years; new cells with new program are needed for ojas.
Ojas is formed in the heart, amrita in the brain; both are physical substances.
The One Self appears as the many (implied by the unity of consciousness and its energy).
The body is the field of yoga; earth element must be recognized in the body.
Time is standstill but appears divided; can be stopped by suspending the sense of time.
Immortality is transformation of future into past is life, absolute transformation is death, unlimited future is immortality.
Kundalini energy rises through Sushumna, piercing chakras, to achieve liberation.
Prana Shakti comes down from Hriday into Muladhara, connects with earth element, awakens Kundalini.
The goal of yoga is to establish connectivity with Mother Earth and merge all elements.
Consciousness is soul; consciousness of the mind is soul.
Knowledge is bondage; the mind limits itself to a body to know itself.
Body is a small bit of power of the creator.
The origin (time) is the basis of knowledge.
Effort to awaken dormant consciousness is terrible (extremely difficult).
Disintegrating consciousness by Pranayam awakens it; one can then create or annihilate worlds.
Direct perception of the fourth state is possible by separating the three states.
Only awakening is knowledge; indecision about dream is ignorance.
Astonishment is the basis for yoga.
Will power is the ultimately beautiful virgin.
The entire visible world is the body of Him who manifests all.
By concentrating into the core, mind knows its invisible form and is established beyond the visible.
Investigation of sublime element causes knowledge of one's abilities.
Deliberation causes self-realization.
There is no difference between worldly pleasures and samadhi – both are awakened consciousness.
Concentration on the void gives knowledge of creation of body.
Concentrating on created things gives knowledge of the five elements and their combinations.
Knowing creation and existence in pure form masters the cycle.
Exploring the great sound reveals the power of time, the origin of all.
Mind is most mysterious; effort is the achiever.
Existence of the present body is a hidden secret.
Dream is realized when growth of consciousness in the womb is distinctive.
When existence in unsupported state becomes natural, status becomes Shiva-like.
Mind taking body, planting seed, developing, and delivering consciousness through death is the Great Way.
When consciousness is born completely, mind no longer needs a body – cycle of birth ends.
Body is an offering; knowledge is food.
When existence is annihilated, it comes forth and experiences the dream.
Mind is soul.
Knowledge is bondage.
Indiscretion about real truth by degrees is illusion.
Merger of degrees within body leads to complete consciousness.
The One Self appears as the many through the medium of the body.
The body is the field of yoga.
Time is standstill but appears divided into phases (childhood, youth, old age).
The unmanifest consciousness (Atman/feeling of beingness) is manifested through the body.
Consciousness always exists, whether manifested or unmanifest.
The same situation appears grievous or pleasant depending on outlook.
Life and death are experienced when consciousness changes its medium of embodiment.
Realizing the relativity of happiness and sorrow leads to immortality.
The conscious mind is a small fraction of total mind; the unconscious is vast dormant consciousness.
Asat (unreal) and Sat (real) are interdependent; yoga awakens the dormant.
The indestructible ultra-brilliant unmanifest Time pervades the entire visible world.
The body is the field of yoga – all battles, events, and places are within the human body.
The one Self (Kutastha Chaitanya) appears as the many (the individual souls and manifest world).
Time is standstill in the inner Self but appears divided due to the mind's activity.
Arjuna's despondency represents the spiritual aspirant's confusion when observing the inner forces of good and evil.
The entire creation is manifested by the unmanifest ultra-brilliant time.
The corporal body is a support for the manifestation of immense consciousness; it is perishable, but the consciousness manifested through embodiment is eternal.
The immense consciousness of the mind is the trans-physical manifestation of time.
Life and death are separate manifestations of one singular presence, experienced due to confinement of consciousness within physical limits.
For those whose consciousness has awakened in its immensity, there is no difference between life and death.
The immense consciousness is eternal, all-pervading, immovable, absolute, and primeval.
One can know the true character of time by awakening dormant consciousness through the body (yoga).
The unmanifest consciousness remains unmanifest for those confined in physical limitations.
The immense consciousness is indivisible, cannot be burnt, wetted, or dried.
The body is a manifestation of unmanifest Time and stores all elements of creation; it can be the medium to know the immense unmanifest consciousness.
The mind itself is like the Supreme Being due to its immensity; senses and organs are its instruments.
No one can remain without performing karma; being born is a consequence of karma, and all are governed by properties inherited from nature.
Suppressing organs without understanding the mind leads to hypocrisy; true control is by regulating the mind.
Yogic practice (sedulous practice of yoga) awakens the immense dormant consciousness of the mind.
The body is both a medium for liberation (via yogic karma) and a cause of bondage (via sensuous pleasures).
The One Self appears as the many through the mind and senses.
The body is the field of yoga; yoga is awakening the dormant immense consciousness.
Time is the unmanifest, brilliant Supreme, yet appears divided in perception.
Yoga is equanimity (samatvam); it is expertise in action (karmasu kaushalam).
Desire for fruits binds one to karma; yoga begins with desire for Samadhi.
Death is a medium to transcend physical limitations and awaken consciousness.
The mind is primary; body and senses are its instruments.
The three gunas limit consciousness; yoga takes one beyond them.
All manifestations are instruments of the Supreme Consciousness for manifesting itself.
The body is the field of yoga; offering the body as oblation in the fires of yoga awakens immense consciousness.
All karma is caused by the body and induced by the mind.
The immense consciousness of the mind is a manifestation of the omnipresent imperishable Supreme Being, which is Time — immutable yet manifested.
The mind sports with itself through itself.
Yogis who have awakened consciousness are above loss and gain, hope and meum.
Those who are not awake cannot understand the advice of the great without first awakening themselves.
By knowing the basic character of death, one unites with the unmanifest consciousness of Time through death.
The vision of Time Conscious persons perceives the play of Time in all creation.
The unmanifest Time (Kāla-Brahma) manifests the entire creation through the medium of the mind.
The immense mind itself envisions all karma and its fruit naturally.
The Time remains beyond karma and its fruit while being the source of all.
The yogi, by awakening dormant consciousness, directly perceives the brilliant Time within.
The one who knows the Time becomes one with the Time and becomes the soul of the world.
Yogic karma emancipates consciousness from physical boundaries.
The result of yogic karma is akarma/Samadhi, a state beyond karma.
The mind is a manifestation of Brahma.
The body is the medium for the yajna of yoga.
Time is the ultimate Supreme Being, imperishable and unmanifest.
All conditions (delight/grief, success/failure) are expressions of one singular consciousness.
Karma is absorbed within the mind itself through yogic practice.
The course of yoga involves systematic granthi-bheda (knot-piercing) stages.
Life and death are different manifestations of one singular presence.
The sustaining power of life is death (Time).
Dharm is the sustaining element; knowledge of death is Dharma.
Desire and anger are born from rajoguna (excessive sense emphasis) and are enemies of awakening dormant mental competencies.
The mind is a manifestation of the imperishable Supreme Being; it is itself a form of the Supreme Being.
The immense consciousness of mind is covered by prominence of body and organs when competencies are dormant.
The unmanifest Parmeshwar (Time) advents as a super-human being to revive yoga in times of decline.
The ultimate truth is Time, which manifests as the visible world and appears in human form.
The human body is the best manifestation of the Time and the field for yoga.
Karma yields quick results in the mortal world, especially yogic karma for awakening consciousness.
The four varnas are stages of evolution of consciousness, not social classes.
Death is liberation for the yogi who knows the true character of Time; for the ignorant, it is destruction and rebirth.
The Time is unaffected by karma and remains beyond bondage.
True knowledge comes from direct perception through yoga, not from reading scriptures.
The body and senses impose physical limitations on consciousness; yoga transcends these limits.
The One Self (unmanifest truth) appears as the many (all creatures).
Time is the Paramatma, the unmanifest truth; knowing its true character liberates.
All karma ends in wisdom; wisdom burns all karma like fire.
Yoga and knowledge (samkhya) are not separate; their fruit is the same.
Samadhi and death are analogous; both yield immensity.
The guru is a medium for the perpetual expression of knowledge, not an individual.
The phenomenal world is a manifestation of the one unmanifest Time (Parameshwar).
True Samnyas is not renunciation of action but knowing death through yoga.
The body is a field that, when cultivated by yoga, yields the crop of immensity (infinite consciousness).
Reality is ultimately the unmanifest Time, which is both the ground and goal of yoga.
All opposites (honor/dishonor, joy/grief, etc.) are manifestations of one singular consciousness.
The True Self is the unmanifest presence beyond the body and mind.
Imagination is the most powerful flow; controlling it by yoga unlocks immense mind capacity.
The mind has immense dormant consciousness beyond the small waking portion.
Yoga awakens the dormant immense consciousness, leading to experience of the Conscious Void.
Practice of yoga yields Vairagya (desirelessness) as a natural result.
A yogi who dies before full awakening crosses physical limitations and is reborn in favorable circumstances.
Consciousness of Time is immanent in the consciousness of the Void.
The One Self appears as the many? Not stated; but 'Void' is the support of all matter and motion.
The body is the field of yoga; the practitioner poises the body and performs special kriyas of head and neck.
Time is standstill but appears divided? Not stated; however 'Vasudev' is the embodiment of Time in the Void.
All desires are fulfilled when the yogi knows the true character of the consciousness of the Void and then knows the consciousness of Time replete in the Void.
Knowledge alone liberates; a yogi is liberated from the attraction of everything he knows.
Continence (Brahmacharya) is a great power; it is attained by restraining the fall of vital fluid (orgasmic discharge) through knowledge of the root cause of attraction between man and woman.
The passion of love is the basis of perpetuation of life; divinity of love was recognized by ancient sages.
Motherhood and fatherhood emerge from love; a woman's body is created for motherhood, and without the cooperation of a man, a woman cannot become a mother.
The one Self appears as the many through the illusion of time and space.
The body is the field of yoga: it supports the mind's consciousness.
Time is standstill but appears divided into past, present, future due to shifting focus of consciousness.
The unmanifest consciousness manifests the immensity of the mind.
The consciousness of the Time is replete in the Conscious Void.
Physical limitations are an illusion (maya) that can be surmounted by surrender to Time (i.e., yoga).
All deities are minute parts of the unmanifest Kala-Brahma.
The conscious matter (Brahma) is the support of the entire creation and has its own day/night cycle of 4.32 billion solar years.
All manifestations originate from the Unmanifest (Time) and merge back into the Conscious Void.
The Conscious Void is a brilliant light with a deep blue center, from which the Anahad Nada emanates.
Beyond the Conscious Void is the Eternal Unmanifest (Time), which does not perish even when all creatures are wiped out.
The ultimate Purusa contains all creatures and manifests the world; reached by undivided faith and valorous yoga practice.
Death can be overcome by realizing one's true form as the brilliance of the Time during the death experience.
The Time (Paramatma) is the ultimate brilliant consciousness that manifests all worlds—gross and subtle.
The one Self (Time) appears as the many (gods, manes, spirits, etc.).
All worship ultimately goes to the Time, whether directly or indirectly.
Yoga is the process of overcoming physical limitations and directly perceiving the Time.
Karma (actions) binds one to cycles of birth and death unless offered to the Time.
The body is the field of yoga; physical limitations are to be transcended.
The Time is uniform in all beings, with no likes or dislikes.
The imperishable unmanifest Time (Brahman) is never spent.
The conscious human mind is the best manifestation of Brahma (self-beingness).
Kriya yoga is the karma that awakens immense consciousness.
The body is mortal and transient but supports immense consciousness.
The conscious mind (Purusa) is a fraction of the immense mind and rules the organs.
Time (Paramatma) manifests the body, mind, intellect, and consciousness.
One attains after death the abstraction (bhava) that dominated the mind at death, which is shaped by lifelong mental habits.
The yogi who directly perceives the unbearable brilliance of Time becomes omniscient.
Repeated practice of Omkar kriya (20736 times per breath) ruptures Brahma-randhra and leads to union with the Immense Brilliance.
Great souls established in the immense brilliance of Time are never reborn.
Time (Mahakala) is unmanifest, without beginning or end, and is the Supreme Lord.
The unmanifest Time manifests all gross and subtle worlds or dimensions.
Time is the support of all supports, the brilliance of all brilliance.
Everything originates from and is sustained by the Time.
The body is an oblation offered in the fires of yoga.
The yogi becomes Time-Conscious through union with the unmanifest Time.
Time bestows knowledge of itself to those who offer their life into the fires of yoga.
The One Self (Time) appears as the many through the conscious Void.
The body is the field of yoga, like earth, with sense organs as its properties.
Time is the ultimate reality, immanent and transcendent, before, during, and after creation.
Creation and dissolution originate from the Time.
The conscious Time motivates self-realization and is the power behind all practices.
The three gunas originate from Time but are only a minute part; Time remains beyond them.
The world is bewildered by the three gunas and cannot know the imperishable Time without yoga.
Yoga stages involve progressively piercing knots: hridaya (heart), rudra (throat?), muladhar (base).
The consciousness of the Void is the intermediate stage; beyond it is the consciousness of Time.
The ultimate goal is to know the true character of the Time and become one with It.
The One Self (Time) appears as the many; the unmanifest manifests creation yet remains aloof.
The body is the field of yoga; the human body is the support for the manifestation of Time.
Time is standstill but appears moving; it is both the doer, action, and consequence.
Time is both creation and destruction; it does nothing yet creates and destroys repeatedly.
The entire creation is contained in the Conscious Void, which is replete in the consciousness of Time.
All spiritual practices (yoga, jñana, mantra, sacrifice) ultimately reveal the same Time consciousness.
Ignorance is equated with sensuous attachment and lack of yogic awakening; knowledge is realization of Time.
The One Supreme Self (Kutastha Chaitanya) appears as the many through the power of Maya.
The body is the field of yoga, and all external events are allegories for inner processes.
Time (Kala) is the ultimate reality, both transcendent and immanent, creator and destroyer.
All beings, from gods to insects, are absorbed into Time, which is the Self.
The gross body is consumed by Time; the subtle essence remains.
The vision of the universal form (Virat) is a direct experience of the unity behind diversity.
The inner brilliance of the Self is beyond comparison with any external light.
The One Self (Time) appears as the many but is ultimately the same consciousness.
The body is the field of yoga, and the mind is the instrument.
Time is the ultimate reality, both frightful and beautiful, immanent in the Void.
Yogic practice leads through stages: hridaya-granthi-bheda, consciousness of the Void, darshan of Vasudev, then direct perception of Time.
Bhakti (undivided dedication) is the means to know, see, and enter the Time.
The idols and deities are not imagination but eidetic hints of the unmanifest Time.
The unmanifest is hard to attain for embodied souls; it requires extra hard work.
All creatures are manifestations of the Time; a Time-Conscious person sees no difference.
The three gunas (sattva, raja, tama) confine the immense consciousness of the mind to the body.
Sattva guna provides an afflatus to raise consciousness above physical limitations and know the true character of Time.
Rajoguna causes intellect to have predilection for senses, entrapping it in greed and confining consciousness to body and organs.
Tamoguna bewilders immense consciousness by enveloping it with ignorance, causing most mental competencies to become dormant.
The Sthitaprajña recognizes the entire creation as a manifestation of the unmanifest imperishable Brahma.
The three gunas have originated from the imperishable Brahma itself.
A person who goes beyond the three gunas attains immortality, becoming free from birth, death, old age, and sorrow.
The true character of death is known through yoga, and one can enjoy immortality by overstepping death.
The body is the field (kṣetra) for yoga, and the mind (kṣetrajña) is the knower of the field.
The One Self (Time/Brahman) appears as the many through the manifestation of Prakṛti.
Time is standstill but appears progressional due to differential levels of consciousness.
The mind is the prime support of all senses; it is eternal because Time is eternal.
Liberation is achieved by awakening dormant consciousness and focusing it on Time.
The accomplished yogi becomes one with the Time, omniscient and omnipresent.
Time (Kāla) is the supreme reality, the destroyer and creator of worlds.
Time creates and destroys everything; destruction is certain from the moment of birth.
All beings are already killed by Time from birth; any killer is just an instrument of Time.
Time is past, present, future; it is beyond them.
Time manifests as all deities and elements (Vāyu, Yama, etc.).
The charming four-armed form of Time is accessible only through yoga after heart-knot piercing.
The terrible universal form of Time is frightening and can only be seen by yogis who have passed all stages of yoga.
Time is the knower, the knowable, and the goal of knowledge.
The world is imbued by Time; Time is the ultimate support and destiny.
The yogi surrenders to Time, igniting the fires of yoga, and seeks Time's love like a father's, friend's, or lover's.
The entire creation is a manifestation of the absolute reality of the Time.
The Time is the creator and destroyer of all; it does everything while doing nothing.
The body (kṣetra) and mind (kṣetrajña) combine to produce the experience of creation.
The unmanifest Time manifests all gunas but remains beyond them.
The Conscious Void contains all conscious matter and yet remains unaffected.
The Time is immanent everywhere; knowing it leads to oneness with it.
Death is to be known and overcome; the Time Conscious are free from the fear of death and rebirth.
The human mind is a manifestation of the imperishable Supreme Being, the Time.
The body is a field (kshetra) for the mind to take experiences and know itself.
The knower of the field (kshetrajna) is the awakened mind, which is the Supreme Being manifested.
All creatures are manifestations of the immense unmanifest Time.
Time is the unmanifest, imperishable Brahma, luminance of all luminance.
By realizing the true character of Time, one becomes immortal beyond death.
The world and all dualities are relative; the enlightened one sees the self in all.
Yogic practice awakens dormant consciousness and leads to knowledge of the Truth.
The entire creation is a manifestation of the Supreme Time (Kāla).
The Supreme Time is the essence behind all names and forms.
All beings and objects are expressions of the One Supreme Self.
The body is the field of yoga and sacrifice.
Time is both the destroyer and the source of all.
The inner yogic process decodes external symbols.
There is no existence without the Supreme Self.
The Supreme Self is both immanent and transcendent.
Divine vision is required to perceive the ultimate form.
The yoga of concentration leads to direct realization.
The unmanifest Time manifests this creation.
The body is limited; dormant consciousness confines one to sense objects.
Lust, anger, and avarice are the three doorways to hell that hide divine immensity.
The scriptures are the experiential records of past sages, preserved for future seekers.
The ultimate destiny is to know the Time and become one with It through yoga.
The unmanifest, ultimate imperishable Truth (Time) is the foundation of all cycles, planets, and constellations.
The brilliance of Time is the source of all brilliance (sun, moon, fire).
Time itself is the juice of all juices (Soma) and manifests all medicaments.
The unmanifest Time (Vasudev) resides in the heart of all beings.
There are two Purusas: the perishable (body/mind) and the imperishable (Kutastha/Vasudev).
The excellent Purusa (Purusottam) is beyond both, the unmanifest Time.
Only Time Conscious persons, through yoga, directly perceive the Time.
The human mind is a manifestation of the imperishable Brahma (Time).
The body is like a tree with roots upward (mind) and branches downward (nerves).
Time is the brilliance that illuminates all luminaries (sun, moon, fire).
The mind's consciousness is eternal and a quotient of the Ultimate Truth.
Ordinary persons are confined to the leaves (desires) and cannot see the whole tree.
By cutting the tree with the weapon of non-attachment (yoga), one reaches the immutable state.
Those who have awakened their consciousness become Time-conscious and never return to embodiment.
The gunas nourish the branches; beyond them lies the neutral Time.
The One Self appears as the many through the manifestation of Time.
The body is the field of yoga; consciousness is confined in physical limitations.
Time is the imperishable Brahma that manifests the entire creation.
Knowledge of the single Imperishable Presence integral in all creatures is sātvik.
Desirelessness and awakening of immense consciousness lead to realization of the Truth.
The five expedients (body-mind, senses, efforts, doer, Vasudev) cause karma and deliverance.
The doer is not the true agent; the five causes together initiate action.
Sātvik knowledge perceives unity, rājasik perceives diversity, tāmasik is trivial and obsessed.
The body is the field of yoga, containing the five elements and the latent Vāsudev (immense consciousness).
Faith (śraddhā) varies according to the level of consciousness; one's actions correspond to that level.
The mind, along with its immense consciousness, is set up on the support of the body.
Scriptures are encoded manuals preserving the wisdom of Time-conscious ancient sages, providing hints for yoga.
The goal is to awaken the immense consciousness that is latent within, transcending physical limitations.
The One Self (Vāsudev) appears as the many due to differing levels of consciousness.
Yoga-karma leads to desirelessness and ultimately to samādhi.
Purity, continence, nonviolence, truthfulness, self-study, and mental calmness are penances (yogic practices).
The Truth is explained symbolically in the scriptures for the benefit of mankind.
The Time is the ultimate Supreme Lord, unmanifest, great, eternal, and unbearable ultra-brilliant.
Awakening the immense consciousness of the mind leads to direct perception of the Time.
The body and senses limit the mind; giving up these limitations allows focus on the Time.
The four duties of life are performed by the body and should be dedicated to the Time.
Bowing before the Time involves uniting past, present, and future consciousness.
The ancient yoga is the means to become dear to the Time.
Ignorance is a sin caused by limited consciousness; the Time releases one from it.
The secret discipline of yoga should not be taught to those who are indolent or opposed.
Spreading this secret knowledge to devotees leads to becoming one with the Time.
The unmanifest Brahma manifests the visible world and is omnipresent as its Creator.
The body is a machine-like embodiment governed by the unmanifest Mind/Time.
The immense consciousness (unmanifest mind) is latent in every human and can be awakened through yoga.
All karma is enveloped by blemish, like fire by smoke, but yoga practice leads beyond it.
True accomplishment is knowing the unmanifest Time and becoming one with It.
The mind impels even a deluded person to practice yoga eventually.
The Time (Parameshwar) governs all beings from the heart by Maya.
The one Self appears as the many through the three gunas.
The body is the field of yoga and the starting point for awakening.
Time is the unmanifest consciousness that stands still but appears divided.
There is nobody on earth or heaven free from the three gunas.
The four varnas correspond to human races and their innate qualities.
Yoga is the process of uniting mind and prana to transcend physical limitations and know death.
True knowledge is knowing the correlation of body, mind, and Time.
The intellect has three levels based on gunas: sattvik (discerning), rajasik (confused), tamasik (deluded).
Holding (dharana) also has three types: sattvik (yogic), rajasik (attached), tamasik (slothful).
Joy has three types: sattvik (poison-then-nectar), rajasik (nectar-then-poison), tamasik (always deluding).
The Puranas, Gita, and Vedas are encrypted manuals of inner yogic process; their symbols, names, places, and events decode into inner yogic meanings.
The one and only truth is the unmanifest Time (Brahma), which is imperishable and beyond mind.
The ultimate unmanifest Brahma, Time, manifests this creation; all three stages of yoga, their practice, and advice are manifestations of the unmanifest Time.
'Om', 'Tat', 'Sat' are directives (indicators) towards the ultimate Truth, preserving the teachings of great yogis who realized the truth of Time.
All desires originate from the mind; knowing this causes desires to sink in the mind.
Death (samnyasa) is rising above physical limitations and awakening the dormant consciousness of the mind.
The body is the field of yoga; the fires of yoga are ignited in the body.
Karma (action) that strengthens physical limitations, performed with desire for sense satisfaction, is Asat.
Sattvic acts (charity, yajna, tapas) purify the learned ones and lead to knowledge of Time.
framework.identity_doctrine: Names and forms (e.g., Krishna, Arjuna, yajna, charity, penance) are not merely historical or literal entities but are symbols for inner yogic processes and states of consciousness. The One unmanifest Time appears as the many through manifestation, yet all manifestations (yoga stages, practices, advice) are essentially the same unmanifest Time. The identity of a thing is determined by its relationship to the inner yogic path: it is either a state of limited consciousness (Asat) or a state of Time-consciousness (Sat). The ultimate identity is the unmanifest Time itself, which is both immanent in the Void and transcendent.
framework.cosmology: Time (unmanifest Brahma) is the ultimate creator, imperishable, beyond the mind. It is the one and only truth. The creation is manifested by this imperishable Brahma. Time is immanent in the Void (the consciousness of the Void). The three stages of yoga, their practice, and advice are manifestations of the unmanifest Time. There is no detailed creation or dissolution cycle described beyond this; the focus is on the inner realization of Time as the ultimate truth, and the process of awakening dormant consciousness by rising above physical limitations. Death is seen as a transition to a greater fear if one is established in Asat, but for the realized, death leads to knowledge of Time.
